replies.text: +Steve Rabon lol - no worries mate - you are quite right about the comments - i try to keep them polite and on topic but i have to balance that with letting people say what they want - not an easy task - anyway - the truism that if you want a good job you use good materials applies here too. If you want a good paint job you use good paint. So, if you want good graphene you have to use good graphite - the best is natural flake graphite in the 45-150 micron range for wet chemical methods. For electrochemical methods the same quality but in chip or lump will work best. Pencils have been ground and mixed with clay binder so you get a lot of rubbish in there and random particle sizes that can cause later problems - hope this helps

replies.author: @suoppsdn9751
replies.text: Yeah I know what you mean. It also helps to try to make it in glass because metals can also change the chemical make up of whatever you put into it. That is why when you walk into a Lab you see glass vials and other stuff that is made of glass. If you try to do a chemical reaction in a metal container then your reaction will also have traces of that metal in the final reaction. I see so many people trying to do backyard chemistry trying to get a pure reaction out of a metal container. Don't get me wrong. I know very little about chemistry but do know if you want a pure reaction you need glass and pure chemicals to fully get a pure reaction.

replies.author: @american665
replies.text: Ok, the substance I was working with was extra fine graphite.  I did several things, the H2O2, the blender, ect.  The yield was small, nevertheless a small amount of graphene was produced with the blender method.  Super thin and kind of hard to work with in solvent.  On another note, I made a very simple small sheet of graphite, and used it in a few applications.  When running electricity through it a 7% to 10% loss was observed.  I also tested this with some of John Hucthinson's battery cells.  This seems to add to the effect of aforementioned cells.  Basically a copper pipe end was treated with a variety of salts as well as other substances.  Then a zinc rod was used for the negative terminal.    The graphite cut mini sheets were wrapped around the zinc and did show improvement.  These cells can be made super small, so small that an array fitted to a triple A battery (five or ten in series) would in theory produce a very long lasting battery.  BTW the failed attempt at graphene via H2O2 method resulted in decanting the substance until completely dry.  The substrate was applied liberally to a workable glue.  Upon drying was a layer of thin graphite that can be cut or laid out wet to construct an easy to make circuit board.  This is my experience thus far.

replies.text: This comment is extremely misleading because only highly pristine graphene can be used in those devices with a conductivity of 700000 S/m instead of only 2000 S/m. I work on bleeding edge 2D nanomaterials and I'm sorry to inform you that the actual 2D materials that will be used for all of the applications you are advocating for are Ti3C2Tx MXene because they are 100 times as conductive and will be priced only twice as much as graphene, and will be 1000X cheaper than than highly pristine graphene when their conductivity is taken into account. They are also the best engineerable materials as 1 layer is 50% more conductive and 50% stronger than graphene meanily because they are 4.5 times thicker and because you can't just glue layers of graphene together. They are also very elastic unlike graphene which crackes under AFM nanoindentation.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: FLGs are stacked like a deck of cards one on top of the other. If you put decks of cards next to each other then there isn't much strength and not much conductivity. Spin coating is more like if you mixed the cards up so there is lots of overlap. Much more strength, much more conductivity. The drying process pretty much automatically stacks the plates correctly.

author: @pogonapog8831
text:
wouldn't the film obtained by spin coat also be FLG? but still thiner than using the drying method right?

also, I'm guessing that in order to obtain graphite from stack of FLG flakes (obtained by eliminating the solvent), the plains of graphine (flakes) should be oriented properly and of the enough size, but because that's not the case, the final solid would have more similarity (in properties) with graphene than graphite (if we do not compress), does that makes sense?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Yes. In the same way that any graphite intercalated compound does. However the structure is vastly different to graphite. If you want graphene you would add a dispersant like pvp to the solution to prevent aggregation of the flakes. You would only dry the solution if you wanted an FLG that you were going to compress for flexible foils or you planned on re intercalating it to further reduce flake thickness. To use the graphene you would leave it in solution and spin coat before drying.
